# Questions in Livestream 37

Live Stream #37: Normal Temp Before Mummification

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction |
|---:|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=260" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">4:20</a> | When Jonah fled from the presence of the Lord, was he specifically fleeing the temple? | Partly in a metaphorical sense, but Falk said Jonah's real motive was refusing the mission to Nineveh, not merely leaving temple space. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=485" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">8:05</a> | How should the word "satan" be understood in Numbers 22? | Here it means an adversary rather than the later proper name for the devil. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=616" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">10:16</a> | Did Pharaoh Necho really receive a message from God, and should Josiah have listened? | Falk said the text reports Necho's claim, not an independent confirmation, though Josiah probably should have avoided the fight on political grounds. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=904" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">15:04</a> | If European scholarship is moving away from the classic documentary hypothesis, is it moving toward unity or even further away? | He said the field is now more fragmented, with the supplementary hypothesis having more traction than a return to simple unified authorship. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=1128" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">18:48</a> | Are the Megiddo stables really Solomonic, and what evidence is for or against that? | No. Falk said the identification came from forcing unrelated texts together, while the ceramic evidence dates the remains much later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=1425" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">23:45</a> | Did Ezekiel 29's scattering of Egyptians happen under the Persians? | He said the best fit is earlier Assyrian deportation policy, though later invasions could also recontextualize the prophecy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=1545" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">25:45</a> | What is his opinion of Maggie Bryson's views on the Exodus? | He respected her as an Egyptologist but said her Exodus comments were not well informed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=1712" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">28:32</a> | Did Moses go to Sheol, and was his shade brought out at the transfiguration? | Yes to both, in Falk's view; he treated Moses as among the righteous dead before the resurrection. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=1918" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">31:58</a> | How many Egyptian kings can be identified from the Bible? | He said several can be identified directly or indirectly, including Shishak and Hophra, with a few others appearing by toponym or title. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2063" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">34:23</a> | What are his favorite bands? | He said his tastes are broad and shaped partly by early work in music retail, rather than by one narrow genre. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2204" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">36:44</a> | Is archaeology partly fueled by the same kind of fantasy represented by River Song and Doctor Who? | Yes, in the harmless sense that archaeologists do dream about big finds, and he added that Doctor Who helped inspire his own interest in archaeology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2382" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">39:42</a> | Are there extra-biblical sources for King Og, and how big does the Bible imply he was? | Falk said he was not aware of extra-biblical sources for Og and did not want to claim more than the evidence allowed. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2446" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">40:46</a> | Would we expect Joseph to appear under his Egyptian name, and are there sites where his name might still be found? | If found at all, it would likely be under his Egyptian name and most plausibly at Avaris, though Falk said the preservation there is so poor that he doubts it will happen. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2715" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:15</a> | Is there extra-biblical evidence for Nebuchadnezzar's madness in Daniel 4? | No known external evidence, and Falk said such a record would be unlikely because ancient regimes would avoid recording that kind of royal disgrace. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=2870" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">47:50</a> | Could Seti I's official Mahi have been Moses, later erased by Ramesses II? | No. Falk rejected the identification on both chronological and etymological grounds. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=3068" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">51:08</a> | Should a student already taking German start Hebrew now or wait for college? | He said starting Hebrew alongside advanced German is workable, especially if the student is already past the beginner stage in the first language. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=3261" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">54:21</a> | When did the divine name Yahweh become too holy to pronounce, and would the prophets have said it aloud? | He placed the shift around the second century BCE and said earlier prophets would have pronounced Yahweh normally. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=3479" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:59</a> | What should we make of claims that Semitic religion was basically Saturn or astrology worship? | He dismissed that as an oversimplification built on conjecture; astral associations exist, but Semitic religion is much more complex than that. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=4081" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:01</a> | How old does he think Moses was during the Exodus? | Falk estimated roughly forty to fifty, treating the three forties in Moses' life as idiomatic long periods rather than exact chronology. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=4252" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:10:52</a> | Is there a Mesopotamian king or dynasty that matches Nimrod's accomplishments in Genesis 10:10-12? | He said possible candidates such as Sargon of Akkad or Naram-Sin have been suggested, but he had not studied the issue enough to give a firm answer. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=4483" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:14:43</a> | How should someone memorize material and focus on studies with exams a few months away? | He advised sleeping enough, reviewing early and often, avoiding last-minute cramming, and focusing on steady work rather than panic over grades. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=4919" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:21:59</a> | Could the Ramesses II scarab from the Mount Ebal altar challenge both an early Exodus date and the documentary hypothesis? | He said it creates problems for both early-date advocates and critical scholars, since the find points toward a late-thirteenth-century setting that neither side easily absorbs. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=5367" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:29:27</a> | Is there a cultural reason Genesis and Exodus name Mesopotamian and Canaanite kings but leave Egyptian pharaohs unnamed? | Yes. Falk said New Kingdom Egyptian texts often call the king simply "Pharaoh," and that usage fits the cultural period reflected in the Exodus story. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=5576" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:32:56</a> | Does Terah being from Urkesh invalidate the use of Ur Kasdim in Genesis? | No. He argued Ur Kasdim may distinguish this Ur from Sumerian Ur and may even preserve a form related to Urkesh. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=5835" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:37:15</a> | Could river course changes explain Ur of Sumer being in Mesopotamia? | Falk doubted it, because a river shift large enough to solve the problem would likely have destroyed the site. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=5884" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:38:04</a> | Why would Terah make such a big deal about moving only about twenty-five miles? | He said the importance was social and political, not just geographic: Terah would have moved from local power broker to displaced outsider. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=6095" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:41:35</a> | Was Abraham royalty? | Falk said Terah looks more like a plutocrat or power broker than a royal-line figure, while Abraham still appears to have inherited substantial resources and retainers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=6277" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:44:37</a> | Was Terah Nimrod's idol maker? | No. Falk said the chronology does not fit, since Nimrod would belong much earlier than Terah. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=6341" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:41</a> | If an Old Testament professor teaches a fifteenth-century BCE Exodus date, should a student correct it or let it be? | He advised the student to avoid unnecessary trouble in class, answer according to the professor's expected view when required, and keep studying the evidence independently. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=6592" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:49:52</a> | How do we know the Merneptah Stele is not referring to Israel suffering famine or defeat by Canaanites? | Falk said the poetic section reflects Merneptah's own campaign activity, with geography and comparanda such as Gezer supporting a direct encounter rather than famine. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=6783" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:53:03</a> | If a primordial singular divine entity was inactive, why did Egyptians come up with an inert deity before active gods? | He explained it as an origin-of-the-gods problem: some Egyptian traditions began with primordial divine essence or waters from which active creator gods emerge. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=7070" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:57:50</a> | When did coins come into Egypt, and what did Egyptians use before coinage? | Coins came in during the Greek/Ptolemaic period; before that, Egypt used barter systems valued by weights such as the bronze deben, along with grain measures. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=7175" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:59:35</a> | What is the Egyptian word for money? | Falk answered "deben," while noting it was a unit of value or weight rather than circulating coinage. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/5O4HOnymvwU?t=7213" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:13</a> | Does he agree with Michael Heiser that Genesis 1 does not purport creation ex nihilo? | Yes. He said Genesis 1 presents fiat creation and a divine ordering of a pre-existing watery setting, not a direct claim of creation out of nothing. |
